title: Why does repairing something change how we feel about it?
summary: Explain why mending an object we could simply replace alters our relationship to it, and what is lost when nothing is ever repaired.
topic: How repairing changes our relationship to the things we own
prompt: Write a 450–600-word essay explaining why repairing something we could have replaced changes how we feel about it. Use at least two concrete examples, acknowledge one case where replacement is the wiser choice, and use cleft sentences, the passive, and hedged generalisation.
hintStarters: There is a strange satisfaction in a mended thing that a new one never gives, and the reason is…
What repair does that replacement cannot is…
Granted, there are objects not worth saving, and insisting on mending them would be…
The act of fixing turns a possession into something closer to…

hintVocab.meaningVi: sự lỗi thời có chủ đích
đáng được cứu / đáng giữ lại
sự hiểu biết tường tận về một đồ vật
công lao động của đôi bàn tay
được cứu vớt / được tận thu
một mối quan hệ chăm nom
sự coi sóc / trông nom có trách nhiệm

sampleText:
Three winters ago the zip on my only good coat gave out on a station platform in Tbilisi. The sensible thing was to buy another coat; a replacement would have cost less than the repair and arrived by the weekend. Instead I carried it to a tiny shop where an elderly tailor spent forty minutes coaxing a new zip into the old lining, muttering at the stitching as though it had personally let him down. I have worn that coat for three winters since. I am, if anything, fonder of it than before it broke — and that small fact contains something worth explaining.

The obvious account is sentimental, but the real mechanism is more interesting. What repair does that replacement cannot is force a relationship. To mend a thing, you have to understand it: how it was put together, where it failed, what it is made of. The tailor knew my coat in a way I never had. By the time something is fixed, it has been examined, handled, and partly understood — and we rarely stay indifferent to what we have come to understand. A new coat is a stranger. A mended coat is an acquaintance with a history you were present for.

There is also the matter of invested effort. We are built to value what has cost us something, and a repair always costs more than the object is strictly worth. That is, in fact, the whole signal. When I chose to fix the coat rather than bin it, I was quietly declaring it worth saving, and the declaration changed how I saw it. The effort did not just restore the coat; it promoted it. Psychologists have a clumsy name for the general tendency — we overvalue what we have laboured on — but the everyday version is simpler. A thing you have fought for is no longer just a thing.

Granted, this can curdle into stubbornness, and it would be foolish to pretend every object deserves rescue. Some things are not worth saving. A cheap toaster whose repair would cost three times its price, a phone deliberately built so that its battery cannot be reached — for these, replacement is not laziness but sense, and a romantic insistence on mending everything is its own kind of waste. The point is not that repair is always right. It is that we have drifted to the opposite extreme, where replacement has become the unthinking default and repair the eccentric exception.

That drift has a hidden cost. When nothing is ever mended, objects stop being companions and become a passing stream — consumed, discarded, forgotten, replaced. We lose the intimate knowledge that comes from fixing, and with it a certain steadiness in our relationship to the material world. A house full of things none of which we could repair is a house full of strangers.

So the change repair works on us is not really about the object at all. What is altered is the owner. To repair is to practise a small, unglamorous form of loyalty — to decide that this particular thing, flawed and failing, is still worth your time. Do that often enough and it stops being a habit about coats and toasters. It becomes a habit about how you treat what is yours.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft to isolate what repair uniquely does
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What repair does that replacement cannot is force a relationship' dùng cleft 'What … is …' với mệnh đề quan hệ lồng ('that replacement cannot') để dồn trọng tâm vào điều mà chỉ việc sửa chữa làm được. 'What is altered is the owner' lặp lại kỹ thuật ở dạng ngắn gọn với thể bị động.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What repair does that replacement cannot is force a relationship' uses a what-cleft with an embedded relative clause ('that replacement cannot') to focus on what only repair achieves. 'What is altered is the owner' repeats the move in a terse, passive form.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Passive chain + hedging for a careful generalisation
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'By the time something is fixed, it has been examined, handled, and partly understood' dùng chuỗi bị động song song để nêu một quy luật chung mà không cần chỉ ra ai làm. 'We are built to value what has cost us something' và 'we overvalue what we have laboured on' dùng câu khái quát có tiết chế, tránh tuyệt đối hóa.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'By the time something is fixed, it has been examined, handled, and partly understood' uses a parallel passive chain to state a general rule without naming an agent. 'We are built to value what has cost us something' and 'we overvalue what we have laboured on' offer measured generalisations rather than absolutes.

sampleTranslationVi:
Cách đây ba mùa đông, cái khóa kéo trên chiếc áo khoác tốt duy nhất của tôi hỏng ngay trên sân ga ở Tbilisi. Điều khôn ngoan là mua một chiếc áo khác; một chiếc mới hẳn sẽ rẻ hơn việc sửa và đến nơi vào cuối tuần. Thay vào đó tôi mang nó đến một cửa tiệm bé xíu nơi một người thợ may già dành bốn mươi phút nhẫn nại luồn một cái khóa kéo mới vào lớp lót cũ, càu nhàu với đường chỉ như thể nó đã đích thân làm ông thất vọng. Kể từ đó tôi đã mặc chiếc áo ấy suốt ba mùa đông. Nếu có gì thì tôi còn quý nó hơn cả trước khi nó hỏng — và cái sự thật nhỏ bé ấy chứa đựng một điều đáng để lý giải.

Cách giải thích hiển nhiên là chuyện tình cảm, nhưng cơ chế thực sự thì thú vị hơn. Điều mà việc sửa chữa làm được mà việc thay mới không thể là buộc nảy sinh một mối quan hệ. Để vá một thứ, bạn phải hiểu nó: nó được ráp lại ra sao, nó hỏng ở đâu, nó được làm bằng gì. Người thợ may hiểu chiếc áo của tôi theo cách mà tôi chưa bao giờ hiểu. Đến lúc một thứ được sửa xong, nó đã được xem xét, cầm nắm và hiểu được phần nào — và ta hiếm khi còn dửng dưng với thứ mà ta đã đi đến chỗ hiểu. Một chiếc áo mới là một người lạ. Một chiếc áo đã vá là một người quen có một bề dày mà bạn đã chứng kiến.

Còn có chuyện công sức đã bỏ ra. Chúng ta được tạo ra để trân trọng thứ đã khiến ta phải trả giá, và một lần sửa luôn tốn kém hơn giá trị thực của đồ vật. Mà đó, thực ra, mới chính là toàn bộ tín hiệu. Khi tôi chọn sửa chiếc áo thay vì vứt nó đi, tôi đang lặng lẽ tuyên bố rằng nó đáng được giữ lại, và lời tuyên bố ấy đã thay đổi cách tôi nhìn nó. Công sức ấy không chỉ phục hồi chiếc áo; nó còn thăng hạng cho chiếc áo. Các nhà tâm lý học có một cái tên vụng về cho khuynh hướng chung này — chúng ta định giá quá cao những thứ ta đã đổ công sức vào — nhưng phiên bản đời thường thì đơn giản hơn. Một thứ mà bạn đã chiến đấu để giữ thì không còn chỉ là một đồ vật nữa.

Công nhận, điều này có thể hóa chua thành sự bướng bỉnh, và sẽ là dại dột nếu giả vờ rằng mọi đồ vật đều xứng đáng được cứu. Có những thứ không đáng giữ lại. Một cái máy nướng bánh mì rẻ tiền mà việc sửa nó tốn gấp ba lần giá của nó, một chiếc điện thoại cố ý được chế tạo sao cho không thể chạm tới pin — với những thứ này, thay mới không phải là lười biếng mà là khôn ngoan, và sự khăng khăng đầy lãng mạn đòi vá víu mọi thứ lại là một kiểu lãng phí của riêng nó. Vấn đề không phải là sửa chữa luôn luôn đúng. Mà là chúng ta đã trôi dạt sang thái cực ngược lại, nơi việc thay mới đã trở thành lựa chọn mặc định không cần suy nghĩ còn việc sửa chữa thành ngoại lệ kỳ quặc.

Sự trôi dạt ấy có một cái giá ngầm. Khi chẳng có gì được vá víu, đồ vật thôi là những người bạn đồng hành và trở thành một dòng chảy thoáng qua — bị tiêu dùng, bị vứt bỏ, bị quên lãng, bị thay thế. Chúng ta đánh mất sự hiểu biết tường tận đến từ việc sửa chữa, và cùng với nó là một sự vững vàng nhất định trong quan hệ của ta với thế giới vật chất. Một ngôi nhà đầy những đồ vật mà chẳng cái nào ta có thể sửa được là một ngôi nhà đầy người lạ.

Vậy nên sự thay đổi mà việc sửa chữa tạo ra nơi chúng ta thật ra chẳng phải về đồ vật chút nào. Thứ bị thay đổi chính là người chủ. Sửa chữa là thực hành một hình thức trung thành nhỏ bé, chẳng hào nhoáng — là quyết định rằng cái thứ cụ thể này, dù khiếm khuyết và đang hỏng, vẫn đáng để bạn bỏ thời gian. Làm điều đó đủ thường xuyên và nó thôi là một thói quen về áo khoác và máy nướng bánh. Nó trở thành một thói quen về cách bạn đối xử với những gì thuộc về mình.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a 'what' cleft to isolate what only repair achieves?
options: Repair forces a relationship which replacement what cannot does it.
What repair does that replacement cannot is force a relationship.
A relationship is what repair forcing that replacement does not cannot.
Forcing a relationship what replacement cannot does that repair is it.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What repair does that replacement cannot is …' is a what-cleft with an embedded relative clause focusing on repair's unique effect.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence concedes the case for replacement fairly?
options: Some people replace things but they are honestly just lazy and wasteful, no offence.
On the contrary, you should never ever replace anything for any reason at all.
Granted, this can curdle into stubbornness, and it would be foolish to pretend every object deserves rescue.
Replacing stuff is okay I guess, whatever, who really cares about old coats anyway.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B concedes that repair can become stubbornness and that some things deserve replacing, in measured language.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option admits a real limit to the repair argument, formally?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses a parallel passive chain to describe the process of mending?
options: Whoever fixes it examines, handles, and they understand the object partly by then.
Examining and handling is what people do when fixing it and understanding partly.
Something fixed by the time people examine handle understand it is partly then.
By the time something is fixed, it has been examined, handled, and partly understood.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'it has been examined, handled, and partly understood' is a parallel passive chain stating the process without naming who does it.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option uses three passive verbs in parallel and reads fluently?

options: Designing a product to fail or become outdated so it must be replaced
A schedule for cleaning a product
Planning to repair a product in advance
Recycling old products into new ones
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Planned obsolescence' is the deliberate design of goods to wear out or become unusable, forcing replacement.

mcQuestions.prompt: According to the essay, why does invested effort change how we value an object?
options: Because repaired objects last longer than new ones
Because we are built to value what has cost us something, so fixing declares it worth saving
Because repairs are always cheaper than replacements
Because old objects are more fashionable
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay says 'We are built to value what has cost us something' and choosing to fix 'declared it worth saving.'
mcQuestions.hint: Reread the paragraph about invested effort and the 'signal'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option keeps a controlled explanatory register?
options: Throwing stuff away is honestly the worst and everyone who does it is basically awful.
Old coats are way better than new ones, that's just obviously a fact for everybody.
A new coat is a stranger. A mended coat is an acquaintance with a history you were present for.
Nobody should ever buy anything new ever again because it's all a total waste, period.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B explains the difference with a precise, evocative parallel and no slang or overstatement.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid 'honestly', 'way better', 'period' — find the measured contrast.

mcQuestions.prompt: In which case does the essay agree that replacement is the wiser choice?
options: A good coat with a broken zip
A grandmother's recipe book
Any object more than ten years old
A cheap toaster whose repair would cost three times its price
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay names 'a cheap toaster whose repair would cost three times its price' as a case where replacement is sense.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the concession paragraph for the specific examples.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay say has become 'the unthinking default'?
options: Replacement
Repair
Recycling
Saving money
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay says 'replacement has become the unthinking default and repair the eccentric exception.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the end of the concession paragraph.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'a relationship of care' refer to in this essay?
options: A romantic relationship between two people
The bond formed by attending to and maintaining an object over time
A contract with a repair shop
A warranty on a product
mcQuestions.explanation: The idea is that repairing builds a caring relationship with objects, unlike consuming and discarding them.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'care' as ongoing attention and maintenance, not romance.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a 'what' cleft with the passive to relocate the real change?
options: The owner is what altering is by the repair of the coat then.
Altering the owner is what the repair what is doing then it does.
What is altered is the owner.
By repair the owner what is altered is changing it the coat then.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What is altered is the owner' is a terse what-cleft with passive 'is altered', shifting focus from object to person.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the short 'What is altered is …'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses parallel '-ed' participles to describe disposable culture?
options: consuming, then discard, also it is forgotten, replacing
to consume, discarded, and they forget, and a replacement
consumes, discards, forgetting, will be replaced soon
consumed, discarded, forgotten, replaced
mcQuestions.explanation: The four past participles ('consumed, discarded, forgotten, replaced') are parallel, giving the list rhythm and force.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for four words sharing the same '-ed' participle form.

options: Impossible to repair
Easy to repair
Expensive to buy
Recently purchased
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Irreparable' means something that cannot be repaired or restored.
mcQuestions.hint: The prefix 'ir-' negates 'reparable' (able to be repaired).

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence hedges its generalisation about human psychology appropriately?
options: Every single human being on earth always values repaired things more, no exceptions.
We are built to value what has cost us something.
Nobody anywhere ever cares about a thing unless they fixed it themselves, ever.
All people without exception completely ignore anything they did not repair personally.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B states a general tendency ('We are built to …') without the sweeping absolutes of the others.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid 'every single', 'nobody', 'all … without exception' — find the measured generalisation.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's final claim about what repair really changes?
options: It changes the object's resale value
It changes the price of new goods
It changes the owner — it becomes a habit about how you treat what is yours
It changes nothing at all
mcQuestions.explanation: The closing argues 'What is altered is the owner' and that repair becomes 'a habit about how you treat what is yours.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the final paragraph's claim about the owner.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best avoids fake balance while still acknowledging both sides?
options: There are pros and cons to repairing and replacing, as we all know.
Repairing and replacing are both fine, which is just how life works really.
On one hand repair, on the other replacement, so it all balances out.
The point is not that repair is always right. It is that we have drifted to the opposite extreme.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B concedes repair is not always right but makes a specific, real point about the cultural drift — not empty balance.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid the lazy 'pros and cons' formulas — find the one with a specific claim.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of something you repaired instead of replacing — or something you threw away that you could have fixed. How did the choice change the way you felt about the object, and what would change if repair were your default rather than your exception?
